6. The Causal Reasoning

Source: vignettes/causal-reasoning.Rmd
causal-reasoning.Rmd
library(mesa)
#> Loading required package: vctrs
#> Loading required package: tibble
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'tibble'
#> The following object is masked from 'package:vctrs':
#> 
#>     data_frame

The other five vignettes teach the mechanics: runes, patterns, verbs, tables. This one is the intellectual home the mechanics serve. mesa exists because a plain R formula cannot say what an epidemiologist means by outcome ~ exposure + confounder — it can only say that three variables sit in some algebraic relationship. The role a variable plays is where the meaning lives, and this vignette is about where that meaning comes from and what it does and does not buy you.

A formula is not a causal claim

stats::formula() knows left-hand side and right-hand side. It has no concept of exposure, confounder, or mediator — those are epidemiological ideas, not syntactic ones, and R has always required the analyst to carry them in their head instead of in the object. mesa’s central move is to make that carrying explicit: a tm is a variable plus its causal role (vignette("terms")), and every layer above it — expansion, fitting, display — reads that role rather than re-deriving intent from position.

This is deliberately modest. Attaching a role to a term does not verify that the role is correct, any more than naming a variable confounder in your own analysis script would. mesa gives the causal reasoning somewhere to live and something to act on; the reasoning itself — is this variable really a confounder, does this DAG actually represent the world — remains the analyst’s.

Association is not causation, but it is not arbitrary either

Austin Bradford Hill’s 1965 address to the Royal Society of Medicine (Hill 1965) is the origin of the modern instinct that an association needs more than a p-value before it is treated as causal. Hill offered nine viewpoints — strength, consistency, specificity, temporality, biological gradient, plausibility, coherence, experiment, and analogy — not as a checklist to be scored, but as considerations that, taken together, make an association more or less credible as a causal one.

mesa’s role vocabulary does not encode Hill’s viewpoints directly — they are arguments made about an association, not properties of a formula. But two of them shape the package’s structure directly:

  • Temporality is why strata (.s()) and random (.r()) are deliberately not covariates: a variable that defines subpopulations, or that groups repeated observations, is not competing with the exposure for a coefficient — it is a fact about the data’s structure that has to be respected before an association is even estimated.
  • Biological gradient and specificity are why a categorical term keeps its levels rather than collapsing to a single coefficient (vignette("mesa")’s "levels" layout, and estimate_interaction()’s per-level estimates) — a dose-response pattern, or an effect that is specific to one subgroup, is invisible in a table that only shows “yes/no.”

Roles as a lightweight stand-in for a graph

Judea Pearl’s do-calculus (Pearl 2010) formalizes the difference between seeing and doing: P(Y | X = x), an observed association, is not in general equal to P(Y | do(X = x)), the effect of an intervention. The tool that makes the difference legible is the causal directed acyclic graph (DAG) — a picture of which variables cause which, from which the confounders, the mediators, and the valid adjustment sets can be read off rather than guessed at.

mesa does not draw or check a DAG (that integration is on the horizon — see the blueprint’s “DAG integration” entry). What it borrows from the do-calculus framework is the vocabulary a DAG produces: exposure, confounder, mediator, interaction are exactly the node-types a causal DAG distinguishes, and a tm’s role is a direct, if unverified, claim about where that term would sit in one. Declaring .c(smoking) is the formula-level equivalent of drawing an arrow from smoking into both the exposure and the outcome; mesa takes the claim on faith and acts on it (adjustment displays, table layout), the same way a regression takes on faith that the formula it is handed is the right one to fit.

Adjustment, mediation, and the total-effect / direct-effect distinction

The most consequential place the role vocabulary shapes actual output is the mediator role. Adjusting for a common cause of exposure and outcome (a confounder) and adjusting for a variable on the causal pathway between them (a mediator) are both “adding a covariate” in ordinary regression syntax — and they answer completely different questions. Conditioning on a mediator blocks part of the very effect under study.

VanderWeele and Robins’s work on mediation (see also VanderWeele’s 2015 text, Explanation in Causal Inference) formalizes this as decomposing a total effect into a direct effect (the exposure’s effect not through the mediator) and an indirect effect (the effect that travels through it). A term wrapped .m() triggers exactly this decomposition in fmls() (vignette("formulas")): instead of one adjusted formula, the family expands into the triad

fmls(mpg ~ .x(wt) + .m(qsec) + hp) |> formula()
#> [[1]]
#> mpg ~ wt + qsec + hp
#> 
#> [[2]]
#> qsec ~ wt
#> 
#> [[3]]
#> mpg ~ wt + qsec

— the total effect with the mediation pathway held open, the exposure’s effect on the mediator, and the mediator’s effect on the outcome in the exposure’s presence. Comparing the exposure’s coefficient across the first and third formulas is what lets an analyst judge how much of the total effect travels through the mediator, rather than accidentally reporting a direct effect while believing it is a total one.

Effect modification is a property of the exposure’s effect, not a coefficient

VanderWeele and Robins (2007) distinguish four types of effect modification based on where the modifying variable sits on the causal DAG relative to the exposure and outcome — a distinction that matters because “the interaction term’s p-value is significant” is a much weaker claim than “the exposure’s effect differs meaningfully across levels of this modifier.”

mesa‘s interaction role (.i()) and estimate_interaction() are built around the second framing. Rather than reporting the interaction coefficient — which describes a difference in a difference, not an effect either subgroup experiences — estimate_interaction() derives the exposure’s own effect within each level of the modifier, from the fitted coefficients’ variance-covariance structure (Figueiras, Domenech-Massons, and Cadarso 1998): within the reference level the effect is the exposure’s own coefficient; within level j it is that coefficient plus the level’s interaction term, with variance var(b_exposure) + var(b_j) + 2 cov(b_exposure, b_j). That is the number a clinician or policymaker can act on — “the effect in men” and “the effect in women” — where the raw interaction coefficient is not.

d <- mtcars
d$cyl <- factor(d$cyl)
mt <-
  fmls(mpg ~ .x(hp) + .i(cyl)) |>
  fit(.fn = lm, data = d, raw = FALSE) |>
  model_table(data = d)
#> Interaction term `cyl` was applied to exposure term `hp`
estimate_interaction(mt, exposure = "hp", interaction = "cyl")
#> # A tibble: 3 × 6
#>   estimate conf_low conf_high p_value  nobs level
#>      <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl> <int> <chr>
#> 1 -0.113    -0.207    -0.0187   0.121    11 4    
#> 2 -0.00761  -0.112     0.0971   0.121     7 6    
#> 3 -0.0142   -0.0481    0.0196   0.121    14 8

What the package does not do

It is worth being explicit about the boundary. mesa organizes causal intent — it makes roles first-class, keeps mediation and effect modification from collapsing into “just another covariate,” and lays the resulting estimates out so the distinctions survive into a published table. It does not:

  • verify that a declared confounder actually blocks a backdoor path,
  • check a DAG for consistency, or
  • adjudicate between competing causal assumptions.

Those remain exactly as much the analyst’s responsibility as they would be without the package. What changes is that the decisions — this is the exposure, this is on the causal pathway, this is a candidate effect modifier — are recorded once, in the formula itself, and every layer above it (expansion, fitting, the published table) inherits them consistently instead of silently re-deriving or contradicting them.

References

  • Hill, A. B. (1965). The Environment and Disease: Association or Causation? Proceedings of the Royal Society of Medicine, 58(5), 295–300. https://doi.org/10.1177/003591576505800503
  • Pearl, J. (2010). An Introduction to Causal Inference. The International Journal of Biostatistics, 6(2), Article 7. https://doi.org/10.2202/1557-4679.1203
  • VanderWeele, T. J., & Robins, J. M. (2007). Four types of effect modification: a classification based on directed acyclic graphs. Epidemiology, 18(5), 561–568. https://doi.org/10.1097/EDE.0b013e318127181b
  • VanderWeele, T. J. (2015). Explanation in Causal Inference: Methods for Mediation and Interaction. Oxford University Press.
  • Figueiras, A., Domenech-Massons, J. M., & Cadarso, C. (1998). Regression models: calculating the confidence interval of effects in the presence of interactions. Statistics in Medicine, 17, 2099–2105.
